content.text: encompasses the authentication, authorization, and connection security practices required to protect AI integrations from credential theft, privilege escalation, supply chain attacks, and man-in-the-middle attacks.
MCP security is built on two fundamental areas:
Verify identity of users/systems accessing MCP tools
Determine what authenticated users can do; prevent privilege escalation
accept tokens that were not explicitly issued for the MCP server
MCP proxy servers using static client IDs
obtain user consent for each dynamically registered client
: Use OAuth 2.1 with proper token validation and PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) for public clients:
Enterprise IdP (Microsoft Entra ID, Okta, Auth0)

: Token must be explicitly issued for your MCP server
- ✅
All MCP communications must use TLS 1.2+
to prevent eavesdropping and man-in-the-middle attacks:
: Clients verify server authenticity before trusting it:

:
1. Fetch metadata file from server
2. Verify signature against known public key
3. If signature matches → trust server
4. If signature invalid → reject connection
: Prevents supply chain attacks (like PyPI or Docker Hub)
: Only connect to MCP servers from
that enforce namespace isolation and signature checks:
: Refuse connections to servers that lack cryptographic verification or aren't on approved allowlist
MCP servers MUST NOT pass raw user tokens

:
- Poor token storage → session hijacking
- Weak token validation → unauthorized access
- No auditing → can't track who accessed what
instead of passing raw user tokens:
Use vault (HashiCorp, AWS Secrets Manager), not env vars
: Reduces token leak risks and removes static secrets that create persistent attack vectors
Always validate that tokens were explicitly issued for your MCP server

# Validate audience claim
token_audience = token.get("aud")
expected_audience = "mcp.weather-server.com"

if token_audience != expected_audience:
    raise Forbidden("Token not issued for this server")
: Never accept upstream tokens (tokens from other services)
: Start with minimal scopes, only request additional privileges when needed:
: Excessive permissions create risk zones for AI tools and agents, especially with broad file/network access
: Restrict tool access by user role, data sensitivity, and context:
Server acts on behalf of user without approval

# Consent flow
def request_client_consent(client_id: str, scopes: list[str]):
    # Show user consent screen
    consent = user_consent_ui(
        client=client_id,
        scopes=scopes
    )
    
    if not consent:
        raise Forbidden("User denied consent")
    
    # Store per-client policy
    policy_db.set(client_id, scopes)
What's the minimum viable security posture for MCP production deployment?
Every remote endpoint requires OAuth 2.1 authentication
Every tool invocation is logged with user identity
OAuth tokens stored centrally, not in client configs
Access scoped through identity provider groups (not per-user)
These five controls don't constitute complete defense, but close the gaps that historically produce the most severe MCP incidents
: Use Okta, Azure AD, Auth0—not custom auth
: Verify ALL inbound requests on every call
: Token must be explicitly issued for THIS server
: Where possible, authenticate both client and server
: Server uses its own scoped tokens
When working with security & auth, following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance.
Start simple and iterate — begin with the minimum viable implementation before adding complexity.
Test with representative inputs — ensure your test cases cover edge cases, not just the happy path.
Monitor performance — track latency, token usage, and error rates in production environments.
Version your prompts and configurations — treat prompt text like source code with proper version control.
Document your design decisions — record why you chose specific approaches for future maintainers.
Plan for failure — implement graceful degradation when the AI component is unavailable or returns unexpected results.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit what the system produces to catch quality drift over time.
These pitfalls frequently trip up developers working with security & auth. Knowing them in advance saves significant debugging time.
Assuming deterministic output — language models are probabilistic; the same input can produce different outputs across calls. Always validate the structure of responses.
Ignoring token limits — exceeding context window limits causes truncation or errors. Track token counts, especially in multi-turn conversations.
Over-engineering early — building complex infrastructure before validating basic functionality wastes effort. Get a working prototype first.
Hardcoding prompts in code — embed prompts in configuration files or databases so non-developers can iterate on them.
Skipping error handling — API calls fail due to rate limits, timeouts, or content policy violations. Always wrap calls in try/catch with appropriate retry logic.
As your usage of security & auth scales, these performance factors become increasingly important.
Latency budget — typical API latency ranges from 1-30 seconds depending on model size and output length. Design your UX around streaming to provide early feedback.
Concurrency limits — each API tier has request-per-minute (RPM) and token-per-minute (TPM) limits. Implement request queuing for burst traffic.
Prompt caching — use the cache_control breakpoint on stable, repeated prompt prefixes to reduce costs by up to 90% and latency by 85%.
Batching — for non-interactive workloads, the Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction.
Model selection — use Claude Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks and Claude Sonnet or Opus for complex reasoning where quality matters more than speed.
